result.markdown:
---
title: "Working with R Objects"
format: 
  revealjs:
      theme: [simple, style.scss]
      auto-stretch: false
editor: source
---

## Types of Objects in R

This week you've learned how to make four different types of objects in R:

- vectors
- matrices
- lists
- dataframes

Let's review each of these so they are fresh in our memory!

## Vectors

Suppose you want to create two vectors:

1. The days of the week
2. The temperature of the water at Pismo each day

Pencil out what code you would write to create these vectors. 

## Creating Vectors

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
days <- c("Monday", 
          "Tuesday", 
          "Wednesday", 
          "Thursday", 
          "Friday", 
          "Saturday", 
          "Sunday")
```
:::

:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
temps <- c(60.1, 
           59.4, 
           60.8, 
           61, 
           60.1, 
           62.1, 
           59.7)
```
:::

:::
:::

. . .

</br>

What would you expect this code to output?

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
typeof(days)
```
:::

:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
typeof(temps)
```
:::


:::
:::

## Matrices

A matrix is a two dimensional vector! Let's assess our understanding of how 
matrices are made. 

What would you expect this code to output?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
matrix(temps, nrow = 2)
```
:::


. . .

</br>


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in matrix(temps, nrow = 2): data length [7] is not a sub-multiple or
multiple of the number of rows [2]
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4]
[1,] 60.1 60.8 60.1 59.7
[2,] 59.4 61.0 62.1 60.1
```


:::
:::


## Lists

Unlike matrices, lists allow the elements to have different data types **and**
different sizes. 

</br>

Here are two ways to make a list:

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_list <- list(days, 
                       temps)
```
:::


:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_list <- list(days = days, 
                       temps = temps)
```
:::

:::
:::

</br>

**How are these lists different?**

## Dataframes

A dataframe is a special type of list---where every element (column) is the
same size. 

</br>

Draw out how the `watertemp_df` would look:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_df <- data.frame(days = days, 
                           temps = temps)
```
:::


## Assessing the Size of the Object

We might want to know how big an object is. 

What would you expect these lines of code to output?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
length(days)
```
:::


</br>


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
length(watertemp_list)
```
:::


</br>


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
length(watertemp_df)
```
:::


## Assessing the Size of a List or Dataframe

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Lists**


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
lengths(watertemp_list)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 days temps 
    7     7 
```


:::
:::


Notice we are using `length`[`s`]{.underline} here, not `length`!

:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.fragment}
**Dataframes**


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
dim(watertemp_df)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 7 2
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
nrow(watertemp_df)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 7
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
ncol(watertemp_df)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 2
```


:::
:::

:::
:::
:::

## Accessing the Elements of an Object

We might want to extract components of an object. 

Pencil out how you would write code to do the following:

- Extract the first two days of the `days` vector.
- Extract the first element of the `watertemps_list` object.
- Extract the second row of the `watertemps_df` object.

## Accessing the Elements of an Object - Solution

- Extract the first two days of the `days` vector.

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
days[1:2]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Monday"  "Tuesday"
```


:::
:::


:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
days[c(1, 2)]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Monday"  "Tuesday"
```


:::
:::

:::
:::

. . .

- Extract the first element of the `watertemps_list` object.

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_list$days
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Monday"    "Tuesday"   "Wednesday" "Thursday"  "Friday"    "Saturday" 
[7] "Sunday"   
```


:::
:::

:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_list[[1]]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Monday"    "Tuesday"   "Wednesday" "Thursday"  "Friday"    "Saturday" 
[7] "Sunday"   
```


:::
:::


:::
:::

## Accessing the Elements of an Object - Solution

- Extract the second row of the `watertemps_df` object.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_df[2, ]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     days temps
2 Tuesday  59.4
```


:::
:::



## Modifying an Object

Suppose we wanted to add a column containing information on the air temperature 
to `watertemp_df`. 

</br>

Here are the air temperatures for each day: 67, 65, 67, 70, 69, 68, 68

</br>

Pencil out how you would add this information as a new column in `watertemp_df`.

## Modifying an Object -- Adding a Column


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_df$airtemp <- c(67, 65, 67, 70, 69, 68, 68)

watertemp_df
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
       days temps airtemp
1    Monday  60.1      67
2   Tuesday  59.4      65
3 Wednesday  60.8      67
4  Thursday  61.0      70
5    Friday  60.1      69
6  Saturday  62.1      68
7    Sunday  59.7      68
```


:::
:::


<!-- Allison: I thought this might be of interest, but also might be too much -->
<!-- ## Changing Column Names -->

<!-- Since the dataframe now has information on **two** types of temperatures, it -->
<!-- feels like our original column name (`temps`) is too vague.  -->

<!-- If we wanted to change the name of the columns, there are two options: -->

<!-- 1. Go back and change the name when we originally made `watertemp_df` -->

<!-- 2. Change the names now -->

<!-- Option 2 would look something like this: -->

<!-- ```{r} -->
<!-- #| code-line-numbers: false -->

<!-- names(watertemp_df) <- c("days", "watertemp", "airtemp") -->
<!-- watertemp_df -->
<!-- ``` -->

## Modifying an Object -- Changing Specific Values

We also learned that we got the water temperature wrong on Thursday and 
Friday. 

On Thursday the water temperature was actually 60.8 and on Friday the
temperature was actually 61.2. 

</br>

Pencil out how you would adjust these water temperature values. 

## Modifying an Object -- Changing Specific Values

- Thursday is row 4 and Friday is row 5
- water temperature is column 2 (named `temps`)

**Option 1:** 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_df[4:5, 2] <- c(60.8, 61.2)
```
:::


**Option 2:** 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_df[4:5, "temps"] <- c(60.8, 61.2)
```
:::


**Option 3:** 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_df$temps[4:5] <- c(60.8, 61.2)
```
:::



## Modifying an Object -- Changing Column Values

We originally store the air and water temperatures in degrees Fahrenheit, but
many people prefer degrees Celsius. 

The formula for converting from Fahrenheit to Celsius is:

$$\text{Celsius} = \frac{5}{9}(\text{Fahrenheit} - 32)$$

Pencil out how you would convert the `temps` and `airtemp` columns to Celsius.

## Modifying an Object -- Changing Column Values

**Option 1:** Adjust the temperatures and save them back in the original column


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_df$temps <- (5/9) * (watertemp_df$temps  - 32)
watertemp_df$airtemp <- (5/9) * (watertemp_df$airtemp  - 32)
```
:::


</br>

**Option 2:** Make a new column with the Celsius temps


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_df$temps_c <- (5/9) * (watertemp_df$temps  - 32)
watertemp_df$airtemp_c <- (5/9) * (watertemp_df$airtemp  - 32)
```
:::


## Assessing Values of an Object

Personally, I won't swim at Pismo if the temperature is below 16 degrees 
(Celsius). How many days in the last week would I have swam?

Pencil out the code you would write to answer this question.

1. What relational comparison will you use?
2. What output will this comparison give you?
2. How will you use this output to find the number of days I would have swam?

## How many days would I have swam?

1. What relational comparison will you use?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
watertemp_df$temps >= 16
```
:::


2. What output will this comparison give you?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE
```


:::
:::


3. How will you use this output to find the number of days I would have swam?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="false"}
sum(watertemp_df$temps >= 16)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 4
```


:::
:::

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
